subject: cryptography
title: How Cryptography Breaks: Attacks and Misuse

keyConcepts.label: Keystream / nonce reuse (the two-time pad)
keyConcepts.microLesson: Every stream cipher — AES-CTR, AES-GCM, ChaCha20 — encrypts by XOR-ing the plaintext with a pseudorandom keystream: $C = M \oplus K$. The keystream is determined by (key, nonce), so if a nonce is EVER reused, two messages get the same $K$ and $C_1 \oplus C_2 = M_1 \oplus M_2$ — the keystream cancels and structure leaks. One known/guessed plaintext ($M_1$) then gives $K = C_1 \oplus M_1$ and hence every other message. The math of AES is untouched; the deployment simply violated 'never reuse a nonce'. Even with unique nonces, AES-GCM has a separate wear-out limit of $2^{36}-32$ bytes per key/nonce pair.

keyConcepts.label: ECB leaks structure (the penguin)
keyConcepts.microLesson: ECB (Electronic Codebook) mode encrypts each block independently with no chaining, so identical plaintext blocks always produce identical ciphertext blocks. Encryption hides the VALUES but not the PATTERN: a bitmap's flat regions stay flat, which is why the famous 'ECB penguin' is still recognizable after encryption. To spot the leak you just count: how many ciphertext blocks share a value, and therefore reveal that their plaintexts were equal. The fix is a randomized/chained mode (CBC, CTR, GCM) where a nonce or previous block makes even identical plaintext blocks encrypt differently.

keyConcepts.label: ECDSA nonce reuse recovers the key
keyConcepts.microLesson: An ECDSA signature is $s = k^{-1}(z + r\,d) \bmod n$, where $d$ is the SECRET private key, $z$ is the message hash, and $k$ is a per-signature random nonce. If the same $k$ is used twice, both signatures share $r$, and $s_1 - s_2 = k^{-1}(z_1 - z_2)$, so $k = (z_1 - z_2)(s_1 - s_2)^{-1} \bmod n$. Once $k$ is known, $d = (s_1 k - z_1)\,r^{-1} \bmod n$ — total compromise. This is the Sony PlayStation 3 break. Ed25519 derives $k$ deterministically from the key and message, so two signatures can never share a nonce and this attack cannot occur.

keyConcepts.label: Textbook-RSA misuse (padding & shared primes)
keyConcepts.microLesson: Raw 'textbook' RSA without padding fails in ways the math itself does not. (a) Small exponent: with $e = 3$ and a small message $m$, if $m^3 < n$ then $c = m^3 \bmod n = m^3$ is never reduced, so an attacker just takes the integer cube root: $m = \sqrt[3]{c}$. (b) Shared prime: if two moduli were generated with a poor random source and share a prime, $\gcd(n_1, n_2) = p$ recovers it by Euclid — factoring BOTH keys instantly. The defenses are OAEP padding (so identical/small messages never yield structured ciphertexts) and good randomness, both provided by any vetted library. Don't roll your own.

lecture.body:
## The math is rarely broken. The *deployment* is.

You have spent this subject building the engines: XOR and $GF(2^8)$ inside AES, modular exponentiation inside RSA, point addition on elliptic curves. Those engines are, as far as anyone knows, sound — nobody factors a 2048-bit modulus or brute-forces AES-256. Yet systems get broken constantly. **Almost never because the algorithm was weak. Almost always because it was *used* wrong.** This capstone is about the arithmetic of that misuse: the handful of small computations an attacker actually runs. If you want the field's engineering wisdom in prose, the gold standard is Soatok's blog, [Dhole Moments](https://soatok.blog/); the four posts cited below are the ones this lesson is built on.

## 1. Reuse a keystream and it evaporates

Every modern stream cipher — AES-CTR, AES-GCM, ChaCha20 — encrypts the same way a one-time pad does: it XORs your message with a pseudorandom **keystream** $K$ generated from (key, nonce). Encryption is $C = M \oplus K$. The keystream is safe *only if the nonce is never repeated under a given key.* Repeat it, and two messages share $K$:

$$C_1 \oplus C_2 = (M_1 \oplus K) \oplus (M_2 \oplus K) = M_1 \oplus M_2.$$

The key **cancels itself out**. Now a single guessed word of $M_1$ (a fixed header, a known greeting) gives $K = C_1 \oplus M_1$, and from there every other message. This is the exact rock WEP and early WPA died on: their nonces (IVs) were too short and cycled, so keystreams recurred. AES-GCM's nonce is only **96 bits**, and — subtly — GCM is *not* message-committing and carries a wear-out limit of $2^{36}-32$ bytes per key/nonce pair; Soatok lays out these sharp edges in ["Comparison of Symmetric Encryption Methods"](https://soatok.blog/2020/07/12/comparison-of-symmetric-encryption-methods/). The number $2^{36}-32 = 68{,}719{,}476{,}704$ isn't fear-mongering — it's a design bound you respect by rotating keys, exactly the way you'd respect a load rating.

## 2. ECB: encryption that keeps the picture

A block cipher only encrypts one block. To encrypt a *message* you need a **mode**. The naive one, **ECB** (Electronic Codebook), encrypts each block independently — and so **identical plaintext blocks become identical ciphertext blocks.** Values are hidden; the *pattern* is not. Encrypt a bitmap with a big flat background and the outline survives: the notorious **"ECB penguin."** Detecting it is pure counting — how many ciphertext blocks repeat, and therefore betray equal plaintexts. The remedy is a randomized or chained mode (CBC, CTR, GCM) where a nonce or the previous block perturbs each block so repeats vanish. Same AES underneath; the mode is the whole story.

## 3. Reuse an ECDSA nonce and you leak the private key

Signatures are where a reused random number is catastrophic. An ECDSA signature on hash $z$ is

$$s = k^{-1}(z + r\,d) \bmod n,$$

where $d$ is your **private key** and $k$ is a fresh random **nonce** each time. Sign two messages with the *same* $k$ and both share $r$; subtract:

$$s_1 - s_2 = k^{-1}(z_1 - z_2) \;\Rightarrow\; k = \frac{z_1 - z_2}{s_1 - s_2} \bmod n.$$

With $k$ in hand, one line finishes the job: $d = (s_1 k - z_1)\,r^{-1} \bmod n$. Your master key, gone. This is precisely how the **Sony PlayStation 3** signing key was extracted — Sony reused $k$. The cure is structural: **Ed25519** computes $k$ *deterministically* from the private key and the message, so distinct messages can never collide on a nonce and this attack is simply impossible. Soatok's ["Guidance for Choosing an Elliptic Curve Signature Algorithm in 2022"](https://soatok.blog/2022/05/19/guidance-for-choosing-an-elliptic-curve-signature-algorithm-in-2022/) is the practical reference for why deterministic signatures win.

## 4. Textbook RSA: the failures aren't in the factoring

RSA is secure because factoring is hard — but 'textbook' RSA with no padding hands out free wins that have nothing to do with factoring.

- **Small exponent, small message.** With $e = 3$ and a small $m$, if $m^3 < n$ then the modular reduction never fires: $c = m^3 \bmod n = m^3$. The attacker just takes an integer **cube root**: $m = \sqrt[3]{c}$. No factoring, no key — arithmetic a calculator does.
- **Shared primes.** Key generation needs *good randomness*. When it's weak, two different moduli can accidentally share a prime, and then $\gcd(n_1, n_2) = p$ (plain Euclid) factors **both** instantly. Internet-wide scans really have found thousands of live keys colliding this way.

The fixes are old and boring: **OAEP padding** so small/identical messages never produce structured ciphertexts, and a real CSPRNG for key generation. Both come standard in any vetted library — which is the whole point of Soatok's ["How To Learn Cryptography as a Programmer"](https://soatok.blog/2020/06/10/how-to-learn-cryptography-as-a-programmer/): *don't roll your own*, and learn by attacking (Cryptopals, CryptoHack) so you can *feel* where these edges are.

## The through-line

Every attack here is small arithmetic — an XOR, a gcd, a cube root, one modular inverse — and every one exploits a *usage* mistake, not a flaw in AES or RSA. That asymmetry is the discipline's core lesson, and it's why Soatok argues in ["Cryptography Engineering Has An Intrinsic Duty of Care"](https://soatok.blog/2026/02/25/cryptography-engineering-has-an-intrinsic-duty-of-care/) that misuse-prone ciphers and side channels are the *designer's* responsibility, not the user's. Do the computations below by hand and you'll never again mistake 'we used AES' for 'we're secure.'

application.summary: Real-world crypto breaks by misuse, not by broken math: reused stream-cipher nonces (WEP/early WPA), ECB structure leakage (the penguin), reused ECDSA signing nonces (the PS3 hack), and unpadded/weak-randomness RSA. Each attack is a tiny hand computation — XOR, gcd, cube root, or one modular inverse.
application.workedExample:
**Recovering an ECDSA private key from a reused nonce.**

Two signatures come from the same signer on a toy curve of order $n = 11$. Both reused the nonce $k$, so they share $r = 5$: $(r, s_1) = (5, 8)$ on message hash $z_1 = 6$, and $(r, s_2) = (5, 7)$ on hash $z_2 = 2$. We know nothing else — but that's enough.

*Step 1 — recover the nonce.* Because $s = k^{-1}(z + rd)$, subtracting kills $d$: $s_1 - s_2 = k^{-1}(z_1 - z_2)$. So
$$k = \frac{z_1 - z_2}{s_1 - s_2} = \frac{6 - 2}{8 - 7} = \frac{4}{1} = 4 \bmod 11.$$

*Step 2 — recover the private key.* From $s_1 = k^{-1}(z_1 + rd)$, solve for $d$: $d = (s_1 k - z_1)\,r^{-1} \bmod n$. Here $s_1 k - z_1 = 8\cdot 4 - 6 = 26 \equiv 4 \pmod{11}$, and $r^{-1} = 5^{-1} = 9 \pmod{11}$ (since $5\cdot 9 = 45 \equiv 1$). So
$$d = 4 \cdot 9 = 36 \equiv 3 \pmod{11}.$$

**The private key is $d = 3$.** Two signatures and one repeated nonce were all it took. This is the Sony PS3 break in miniature — and Ed25519's deterministic nonces make the attack impossible by construction.

*Further reading:* the definitive engineering treatment of these failure modes is Soatok's [Dhole Moments](https://soatok.blog/) — especially ["Guidance for Choosing an Elliptic Curve Signature Algorithm in 2022"](https://soatok.blog/2022/05/19/guidance-for-choosing-an-elliptic-curve-signature-algorithm-in-2022/).
domainNotes: **Wi-Fi (WEP / early WPA):** short, cycling IVs made the RC4 keystream repeat, so $C_1 \oplus C_2 = M_1 \oplus M_2$ recovered traffic — the exact two-time-pad failure. WPA2/WPA3 (AES-CCMP/GCMP) fixed the nonce handling.

**Game consoles (Sony PS3):** the ECDSA signing key was extracted because Sony reused the nonce $k$ across signatures — the identical computation you did above, at real key sizes. It let anyone sign code as Sony.

**RSA in the wild:** internet-wide scans (e.g. the 2012 'Mining Your Ps and Qs' study) found thousands of live TLS/SSH keys sharing a prime because embedded devices had weak entropy at boot — every one factorable by a single gcd.

**CTF / security research:** 'crypto' categories at CTFs are almost entirely these misuse patterns — nonce reuse, ECB detection, unpadded RSA, small-$e$ cube roots. Practicing on Cryptopals and CryptoHack is how professionals build the instinct.

**Further reading:** Soatok's [Dhole Moments](https://soatok.blog/) is the field's most-recommended source of practical crypto-engineering wisdom — start with 'How To Learn Cryptography as a Programmer' and the symmetric-methods comparison, then 'Cryptography Engineering Has An Intrinsic Duty of Care'.

problems.prompt: A stream cipher reused its keystream nibble $K$ on two messages: $C_1 = M_1 \oplus K = 12$ and $C_2 = M_2 \oplus K = 9$ (4-bit values). You know $M_1 = 9$. Recover the key. (Decimal.)

steps.instruction: Compute $C_1 \oplus C_2 = 12 \oplus 9$ (this equals $M_1 \oplus M_2$).
Recover the key: $K = C_1 \oplus M_1 = 12 \oplus 9$.

problems.solutionNarrative: Reusing one keystream makes $C_1 \oplus C_2 = 5 = M_1 \oplus M_2$. A known message peels off the key: $K = 12 \oplus 9 = 5$.

problems.prompt: AES-CTR was run twice with the SAME nonce, so both messages were XORed with the same keystream byte $K$: $C_1 = 226$ and $C_2 = 195$. A predictable header reveals $M_1 = 72$. Recover the key byte and the second message. (Decimal.)

steps.instruction: Compute $C_1 \oplus C_2 = 226 \oplus 195$ (which is $M_1 \oplus M_2$).
Recover the key: $K = C_1 \oplus M_1 = 226 \oplus 72$.
Recover the second message: $M_2 = C_2 \oplus K = 195 \oplus 170$.

problems.solutionNarrative: The reused keystream cancels: $C_1 \oplus C_2 = 33$. Then $K = 226 \oplus 72 = 170$ and $M_2 = 195 \oplus 170 = 105$. Nonce reuse turns CTR into a broken two-time pad.

problems.prompt: Two AES-GCM ciphertexts share a nonce: $C_1 = 226$, $C_2 = 195$; a known header gives $M_1 = 72$. Break the pair, then recall GCM's SEPARATE safety limit of $2^{36}-32$ bytes per key/nonce pair. (Decimal.)

steps.instruction: Cancel the keystream: $C_1 \oplus C_2 = 226 \oplus 195$.
steps.hint: This is $M_1 \oplus M_2$; the shared keystream drops out.

steps.instruction: Recover the second message directly: $M_2 = M_1 \oplus (C_1 \oplus C_2) = 72 \oplus 33$.
Recover the keystream byte $K = C_1 \oplus M_1 = 226 \oplus 72$.
steps.hint: Once $M_1$ is known, $C_1 \oplus M_1$ strips the mask.

steps.instruction: The wear-out limit is $2^{36} - 32$ bytes. Compute that number.

problems.solutionNarrative: $C_1 \oplus C_2 = 33 = M_1 \oplus M_2$, so $M_2 = 72 \oplus 33 = 105$ and $K = 226 \oplus 72 = 170$. Even with unique nonces, GCM wears out at $2^{36}-32 = 68{,}719{,}476{,}704$ bytes per key/nonce pair (Soatok, "Comparison of Symmetric Encryption Methods").

problems.prompt: In ECB mode, equal plaintext blocks encrypt to equal ciphertext blocks. A 4-block message produced these ciphertext blocks: c1=9F, c2=3C, c3=9F, c4=D1. (Same hex value = same plaintext block.)

steps.instruction: How many DISTINCT ciphertext block values appear?
The value 9F appears twice. How many of the 4 blocks are proven identical to another? (Count both copies.)

problems.solutionNarrative: The repeated 9F betrays two identical plaintext blocks — 3 distinct values across 4 blocks. ECB never hides where a message repeats itself.

problems.prompt: An image encrypted with AES in ECB mode gives this 6-block ciphertext stream (by block value): A1 7E A1 7E C4 A1. Identical values mean identical plaintext blocks.

steps.instruction: How many DISTINCT block values are there?
A1 appears 3 times and 7E appears 2 times. How many blocks belong to a repeated group? (3 + 2.)
steps.hint: Three copies of A1 plus two of 7E.

steps.instruction: How many blocks are UNIQUE (appear exactly once)? (Only C4.)

problems.solutionNarrative: 3 distinct values; A1 (×3) and 7E (×2) put 5 of the 6 blocks into visible repeat groups, only C4 unique. This is why the 'ECB penguin' stays recognizable.

problems.prompt: A 16-block bitmap has a flat background: 10 of its blocks are byte-identical (same solid color), and the remaining 6 foreground blocks are all different from each other and from the background. It is encrypted with AES-ECB.

steps.instruction: The 10 identical background blocks all encrypt to the SAME value. How many distinct ciphertext values does the full image have?
steps.hint: One value for the whole background, plus 6 distinct foreground values.

steps.instruction: How many of the 16 ciphertext blocks are visibly part of the background repeat group?
A secure (randomized) mode would make all 16 blocks look distinct. How many MORE distinct values is that than ECB gives? (16 minus your first answer.)

problems.solutionNarrative: ECB collapses the 10-block background to one repeated ciphertext value, giving 7 distinct blocks instead of 16. The image outline survives encryption — a structural failure, not a math bug in AES.

problems.prompt: Two ECDSA signatures (order $n = 11$) reused the same nonce $k$, so they share $r = 5$: $(r, s_1) = (5, 8)$ on hash $z_1 = 6$, and $(r, s_2) = (5, 7)$ on hash $z_2 = 2$. Recover the nonce $k = (z_1 - z_2)(s_1 - s_2)^{-1} \bmod 11$.

steps.instruction: Compute $z_1 - z_2 \bmod 11$ (i.e. $6 - 2$).
Compute $s_1 - s_2 \bmod 11$ (i.e. $8 - 7$).

problems.solutionNarrative: Since $s = k^{-1}(z + rd)$, the difference $s_1 - s_2 = k^{-1}(z_1 - z_2)$, so $k = 4/1 = 4 \bmod 11$. A reused nonce hands the attacker $k$ for free.

problems.prompt: Finish the PS3-style break from s07. On order $n = 11$ with $r = 5$, $s_1 = 8$, $z_1 = 6$, and the recovered nonce $k = 4$, extract the PRIVATE key $d = (s_1 k - z_1)\,r^{-1} \bmod 11$.

steps.instruction: Compute $s_1 k - z_1 \bmod 11$ (i.e. $8 \cdot 4 - 6$).
Recover $d = (s_1 k - z_1)\,r^{-1} = 4 \cdot 9 \bmod 11$.

problems.solutionNarrative: From $s_1 = k^{-1}(z_1 + rd)$, $d = (s_1 k - z_1)/r = 4 \cdot 9 = 36 \equiv 3 \pmod{11}$. The private key is $d = 3$ — exactly how Sony's PS3 signing key leaked.

problems.prompt: Full ECDSA key recovery on order $n = 17$. Two signatures reuse the nonce, sharing $r = 9$: $s_1 = 1$ on $z_1 = 10$, $s_2 = 10$ on $z_2 = 4$. Recover $k$ and $d$, then note the fix.

steps.instruction: Compute $z_1 - z_2 \bmod 17$ (i.e. $10 - 4$).
Compute $s_1 - s_2 \bmod 17$ (i.e. $1 - 10$, add 17 since it is negative).
Recover $d = (s_1 k - z_1)\,r^{-1} \bmod 17$. Here $s_1 k - z_1 = 1\cdot5 - 10 \equiv 12$ and $r^{-1} = 9^{-1} = 2$. Compute $12 \cdot 2 \bmod 17$.
Ed25519 derives $k$ deterministically, so two signatures cannot share a nonce. Under Ed25519, how many private keys does this attack recover?
steps.hint: Deterministic nonces make the reuse condition impossible.

problems.solutionNarrative: $k = 6/8 = 5 \bmod 17$, then $d = (1\cdot5 - 10)/9 = 12 \cdot 2 = 24 \equiv 7 \pmod{17}$ — total compromise from one repeated nonce. Ed25519's deterministic nonces recover 0 keys, which is why Soatok's ECC guidance recommends it over ECDSA.

problems.prompt: A system uses textbook RSA with public exponent $e = 3$ and NO padding. A small message $m$ was encrypted as $c = m^3 \bmod n$, but $m$ was so small that $m^3 < n$ — so no wraparound happened and $c = 64$ exactly. Recover $m$.

steps.instruction: Since $c = m^3$ was never reduced, recover $m$ as the integer cube root of $c = 64$. What is $m$?

problems.solutionNarrative: With $e = 3$, no padding, and $m^3 < n$, the ciphertext is a plain cube: $m = \sqrt[3]{64} = 4$. OAEP padding exists precisely to stop this (Soatok, "How To Learn Cryptography as a Programmer").

problems.prompt: Same small-$e$ flaw, different message: with $e = 3$, no padding, and $m^3 < n$, an unpadded ciphertext $c = 343$ arrives. Recover $m$.

steps.instruction: Take the integer cube root of $c = 343$. What is $m$?

problems.solutionNarrative: $m = \sqrt[3]{343} = 7$. When the message is small enough that $m^e$ never wraps mod $n$, encryption does nothing an attacker can't trivially undo.

problems.prompt: Two RSA public moduli were generated with a weak random source and accidentally SHARE a prime factor: $n_1 = 77$ and $n_2 = 91$. Anyone can factor both with one gcd. Recover the shared prime $p = \gcd(n_1, n_2)$.

steps.instruction: Run Euclid: compute $91 \bmod 77$.
Continue: compute $77 \bmod 14$.
One more: $14 \bmod 7 = 0$, so what is $\gcd(77, 91)$?
steps.hint: The last nonzero remainder is the gcd.

steps.instruction: Factor $n_1 = 77$ with the recovered prime: what is the other factor $q_1 = 77 / 7$?

problems.solutionNarrative: $\gcd(77, 91) = 7$ instantly factors both keys: $n_1 = 7 \times 11$. Real internet scans found thousands of live keys sharing primes — a randomness failure, not a break of RSA's math.

problems.prompt: Two RSA moduli share a prime: $n_1 = 143$, $n_2 = 187$. The first key publishes exponent $e = 7$. Recover the shared prime, then the full PRIVATE key $d$ for $n_1$.

steps.instruction: Compute the shared prime $p = \gcd(143, 187)$.
Factor $n_1$: the other prime is $q_1 = 143 / 11$. What is $q_1$?
Compute the secret $\varphi(n_1) = (p-1)(q_1-1) = 10 \cdot 12$.
Recover the private key $d = 7^{-1} \bmod 120$.

problems.solutionNarrative: One gcd factors $n_1 = 11 \times 13$, giving $\varphi = 120$ and $d = 7^{-1} \bmod 120 = 103$ — the whole private key. Don't roll your own RSA: use vetted libraries with OAEP (Soatok, "Cryptography Engineering Has An Intrinsic Duty of Care").

problems.prompt: Abstract two-time pad. Two ciphertext bytes made with the SAME keystream $K$ are $C_1 = 200$ and $C_2 = 156$; a crib gives $M_1 = 65$. Recover $K$ and $M_2$. (Decimal.)

steps.instruction: Recover the keystream: $K = C_1 \oplus M_1 = 200 \oplus 65$.
Recover the second message: $M_2 = C_2 \oplus K = 156 \oplus 137$.

problems.solutionNarrative: $K = 200 \oplus 65 = 137$, then $M_2 = 156 \oplus 137 = 21$. Any known plaintext under a reused keystream unravels the rest.

problems.prompt: A 5-block ECB ciphertext stream (by block value) is: 4B 4B 4B 90 4B. Identical values came from identical plaintext blocks.

steps.instruction: How many DISTINCT block values appear?
How many of the 5 blocks share the value 4B?

problems.solutionNarrative: Four of five blocks are the identical value 4B, so ECB reveals that four plaintext blocks were equal — only 2 distinct values total.

problems.prompt: Textbook RSA, $e = 3$, no padding. An unpadded small-message ciphertext $c = 1331$ arrives with $m^3 < n$. Recover $m$, then confirm the flaw's condition.

steps.instruction: Take the integer cube root of $c = 1331$. What is $m$?
The attack works only when $m^3$ stays below $n$. For $m = 11$, what is $m^3$ (the smallest $n$ must exceed for the flaw)?

problems.solutionNarrative: $m = \sqrt[3]{1331} = 11$. The cube-root attack needs $m^3 = 1331 < n$; padding (OAEP) enlarges and randomizes $m$ so $m^e$ always wraps and this shortcut vanishes.
